attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with severe watery diarrhoea, abdominal cramps, nausea, and vomiting for the past 18 hours. She reports eating raw oysters at a seaside restaurant yesterday evening. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She works as an accountant and has not travelled recently.

On examination, she appears unwell and dehydrated. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 100/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.2°C. Her abdomen is diffusely tender but soft with normal bowel sounds. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely implicated pathogen?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation and history strongly suggest Vibrio parahaemolyticus infection. This halophilic bacterium is commonly found in coastal waters and raw shellfish, particularly oysters. The short incubation period of 12-24 hours and the combination of watery diarrhoea, abdominal cramps, nausea, and vomiting are characteristic.

Norovirus can also cause acute gastroenteritis after consuming raw shellfish, but typically has a shorter incubation period (6-12 hours) and is more commonly associated with outbreaks affecting multiple people.

Salmonella enteritidis is more commonly associated with consumption of undercooked eggs and poultry. While it can cause similar symptoms, the incubation period is typically longer (24-72 hours).

Entamoeba histolytica causes amoebic dysentery, which typically presents with bloody diarrhoea and has a much longer incubation period. It is also more commonly associated with travel to endemic areas.

Campylobacter jejuni infection is more commonly associated with undercooked poultry and unpasteurised milk. It typically has a longer incubation period (2-5 days) and often causes bloody diarrhoea.

The key to diagnosis in this case is the combination of recent raw oyster consumption, the appropriate incubation period, and the characteristic clinical presentation of watery diarrhoea with systemic symptoms.
learningPoints: Vibrio parahaemolyticus typically causes gastroenteritis after consumption of raw or undercooked seafood.
The incubation period for Vibrio parahaemolyticus infection is usually 12-24 hours.
Clinical features include watery diarrhoea, abdominal cramps, nausea, and vomiting.
references: Humphries RM, Linscott AJ. Practical Guidance for Clinical Microbiology Laboratories: Diagnosis of Bacterial Gastroenteritis. Clin Microbiol Rev. 2015;28(1):3-31. doi:10.1128/CMR.00073-14
Meisenheimer ES MD, MBA, Epstein C DO, Thiel D MD, MPH. Acute Diarrhea in Adults. Am Fam Physician. 2022;106(1):72-80.
